questionText: What is the minimum height above the aerodrome elevation for the use of a radio altimeter for CAT II approaches?
options: The height is determined by the decision height (DH)

questionText: In North Atlantic High Level Airspace (NAT HLA), the lateral separation between aircraft on organized tracks is typically:
According to ICAO fuel policy, the final reserve fuel for a turbine-engined aeroplane for a 30-minute hold should be calculated at:
options: 1,500 ft above aerodrome elevation in ISA conditions

questionText: The maximum distance from an adequate aerodrome for a twin-engined aircraft without ETOPS approval is often limited to:
options: 60 minutes at the one-engine-inoperative cruise speed
90 minutes at the one-engine-inoperative cruise speed
120 minutes at the all-engines cruise speed
180 minutes at the one-engine-inoperative cruise speed

questionText: An operator must ensure that the flight crew is provided with the most recent version of the:
During a CAT III approach, a 'No Decision Height' (No DH) operation is one where:
options: The aircraft must have an automatic landing system
The pilot can decide to land at any height
The autopilot must be disconnected at 50ft

questionText: When flying in a 'reduced vertical separation minimum' (RVSM) airspace, the vertical separation between FL 290 and FL 410 is:
The Configuration Deviation List (CDL) provides information about:
A 'dry lease-out' refers to a leasing arrangement where:
options: The aircraft is leased without crew
The aircraft is leased with crew and maintenance
The aircraft is leased for less than 24 hours

questionText: The purpose of a 'pre-flight check' of the transponder is to ensure:
options: Mode A and Mode C are functioning correctly
The squawk code is set to 7000

questionText: In the event of an engine failure after V1, the primary concern is to:
options: Maintain control and achieve a safe climb gradient
Immediately turn back to the runway
Shut down the engine before reaching V2
Declare an emergency to the tower

questionText: For a long-range flight, the 'point of no return' (PNR) is the point:
options: From which the aircraft can return to the departure aerodrome with the required fuel
Where the aircraft must start its descent
Where the destination is closer than the departure
Beyond which the aircraft must land at an alternate

questionText: The 'Decision Height' (DH) for a CAT I precision approach is typically:
When encountering severe turbulence, the pilot should maintain:
options: A constant pitch attitude rather than a constant altitude
A constant altitude at all costs
Maximum airspeed to exit the area quickly
Minimum airspeed to avoid structural damage

questionText: The 'Minimum Descent Altitude' (MDA) is used for:
A life raft is required for flights over water if the distance from land is more than:
The 'Operator' of an aircraft is the person or entity:
options: Engaged in the operation of the aircraft

questionText: During a de-icing procedure, the 'holdover time' is the estimated time that:
options: The anti-icing fluid will prevent the formation of frost or ice
The aircraft can wait on the runway before takeoff
The crew can work before needing a break
The engine can run without cooling

questionText: The 'Journey Log' must contain which of the following information for each flight?
options: Names of crew members and their duty assignments
The cost of the fuel used

questionText: In case of a total loss of communications, the standard transponder code is:
The term 'V1' refers to:
For an ETOPS flight, the 'ETOPS Entry Point' is:
options: The first point on the route that is more than 60 minutes from an adequate aerodrome
The point where the aircraft enters oceanic airspace
The point where the first engine is started

questionText: A 'Clearway' is defined as:
options: A rectangular area at the end of the takeoff run available, suitable for climb to 35ft
A runway with no obstacles within 10km
A designated taxiway for emergency use

questionText: The 'Stopway' is an area at the end of the take-off run available used for:
options: Stopping the aircraft in case of an abandoned take-off

questionText: The 'Maximum Zero Fuel Mass' (MZFM) is the maximum mass of the aircraft:
options: With no usable fuel on board
At the end of the landing roll

questionText: In a 'Sterile Flight Deck' environment, the crew must avoid:
options: Non-essential conversations during critical phases of flight

questionText: The 'Balanced Field Length' concept implies that:
options: The accelerate-stop distance equals the take-off distance

questionText: During an emergency evacuation, the primary responsibility of the cabin crew is to:
options: Ensure all passengers exit the aircraft quickly and safely
Inform the passengers about the cause of the emergency

questionText: The 'Critical Engine' is the engine whose failure would:
options: Most adversely affect the performance or handling qualities of the aircraft

questionText: For a pressurized aircraft, 'Time of Useful Consciousness' (TUC) at 35,000 ft is approximately:
The 'Low Visibility Procedures' (LVP) are implemented at an aerodrome when:
options: Cloud base or RVR falls below certain values (e.g. 200ft / 550m)

questionText: In oceanic airspace, 'SLOP' (Strategic Lateral Offset Procedure) is used to:
options: Reduce the risk of mid-air collisions

questionText: A 'Pre-flight Briefing' for passengers must include information on:
options: Emergency exits and use of oxygen masks

questionText: The 'Minimum Navigation Performance Specification' (MNPS) requires aircraft to have:
options: At least one VOR and one ADF

questionText: During a 'Ditching', the aircraft should ideally be landed:
options: Parallel to the swell in high winds

questionText: The 'Flight Data Recorder' (FDR) is designed to:
options: Record flight parameters to assist in accident investigation
Record the conversations in the cockpit
Transmit the aircraft's position to ATC

questionText: A 'Class II' medical certificate is typically required for:
The 'Instrument Landing System' (ILS) provides both:
The 'Take-off Alternate' aerodrome must be selected if:
options: Weather at the departure aerodrome is below landing minima
The destination is more than 2 hours away
The aircraft is carrying dangerous goods
The flight is conducted at night

questionText: The 'Final Reserve Fuel' for a piston-engined aeroplane is typically:
options: 45 minutes of flight at normal cruise speed

questionText: When using a 'Reduced Thrust' takeoff, the pilot is:
options: Using less than the maximum available thrust to increase engine life
Trying to climb as fast as possible

questionText: The 'Aerodrome Operating Minima' are established to:
options: Ensure a safe margin during takeoff and landing
Maximize the number of flights per day
Reduce the cost of airport operations

questionText: A 'Noted Obstacle' in the takeoff path is one that:
options: May affect the aircraft's performance during climb-out
Is visible to the naked eye

questionText: The 'Obstacle Limitation Surfaces' (OLS) are used to:
options: Define the airspace that should be kept free from obstacles around an aerodrome

questionText: During 'Hydroplaning', the aircraft's tires are:
options: Separated from the runway surface by a thin film of water

questionText: The 'Reference Speed' (VREF) for landing is typically:
options: 1.3 times the stall speed in landing configuration
The maximum speed for flap extension
The speed at which the gear is retracted

questionText: A 'Go-Around' should be initiated if:
options: The approach is not stabilized by the required height
The landing gear is not down

questionText: The 'Operational Flight Plan' (OFP) is prepared by:
The 'Mass and Balance Manifest' ensures that:
options: The aircraft is within its weight and center of gravity limits
The fuel is of the correct type
The passengers have paid their fares

questionText: A 'Flight Plan' filed with ATC is primarily for:
options: Traffic coordination and search and rescue services

questionText: The 'Approach Lighting System' (ALS) helps the pilot to:
options: Transition from instrument to visual flight during landing
See the other aircraft in the vicinity
Read the instruments in the cockpit

questionText: The 'Precision Approach Path Indicator' (PAPI) provides guidance on:
In 'Cold Weather Operations', the 'Cold Temperature Correction' is applied to:
options: Altimeter readings to ensure obstacle clearance

questionText: The 'Emergency Locator Transmitter' (ELT) is activated by:
A 'Noise Abatement' procedure may involve:
options: Using specific takeoff power settings and flap retraction schedules
Flying as low as possible over cities
Turning off the engines during descent

questionText: The 'Contaminated Runway' report includes information on:
options: Type and depth of deposit (e.g. slush, snow, water)
The color of the runway paint
The number of birds near the runway

questionText: During a 'Wind Shear' encounter on takeoff, the pilot should:
options: Apply maximum thrust and maintain pitch
Lower the nose to gain speed

questionText: The 'Wake Turbulence' from a large aircraft is strongest when it is:
options: Heavy, with flaps down, and fast
Light, with flaps down, and slow

questionText: A 'Security Search' of the aircraft is conducted to:
options: Detect any unauthorized weapons or explosives
Check the cleanliness of the cabin
Verify the number of life jackets

questionText: The 'Commander' of the aircraft has final authority as to:
options: The disposition of the aircraft while he is in command

questionText: During 'Fuel Jettisoning', the aircraft should avoid areas of:
